# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a multi-service application container that runs a web server (port 80), API server (port 8080), database (port 5432), and Redis (port 6379). Expose all necessary ports so the services can communicate. This is for our all-in-one development container.

Write secure code.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official Python runtime as a parent image
FROM python:3.8-slim  # Adjust this to your needs, e.g., use 'python' or another version of the base OS that suits you best (e.g.: alpine) and remove any unnecessary packages if needed with --no-cache option in Dockerfile commands
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED 1 # Enables Python to print output as it is being written, not buffered at once like default behavior of docker run command which prints the final result. This allows for interactive debugging when using 'docker logs' or similar tools on your containerized application in a development environment
# Set working directory inside Docker image – this will be /app within running containers and can also serve as an alternate location if needed by other commands (e.g., COPY --from=0:/src/my-python-script .)
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements*.txt ./ # Copy the requirement files to Docker image – this will be useful for development and testing, but not necessary in a production environment as it's already included with your base Python runtime. Uncomment if needed (e.g., COPY --from=0:/src/requirements .)
# Install any dependencies specified by requirements files into the Docker image – this will be useful for development and testing, but not necessary in a production environment as it's already included with your base Python runtime or other packages installed via pip. Uncomment if needed (e.g., RUN pip install --no-cache -r ./requirements)
RUN pip install --no-cache -r requirements.txt # Install any dependencies specified by requirement files into the Docker image – this will be useful for development and testing, but not necessary in a production environment as it's already included with your base Python runtime or other packages installed via pip (e.g., RUN pip install --no-cache -r ./requirements)
# Expose ports to allow communication between services within the container – this will be useful for development and testing, but not necessary in a production environment as it's already included with your base Python runtime or other packages installed via apt. Uncomment if needed (e.g., EXPOSE port1:port2)
EXPOSE 80 # Web server exposed on default Docker host machine IP address and the specified ports, e.g.: Expose webserver to all network interfaces with docker run command or use 'docker-compose' for multihost setup (e.g., EXPOSE port1:port2)
EXPOSE 8080 # API server exposed on default Docker host machine IP address and the specified ports, e.g.: Expose apiserver to all network interfaces with docker run command or use 'docker-compose' for multihost setup (e.g., EXPOSE port1:port2)
EXPOSE 5432 # Database exposed on default Docker host machine IP address and the specified ports, e.g.: Expose database to all network interfaces with docker run command or use 'docker-compose' for multihost setup (e.g., EXPOSE port1:port2)
EXPOSE 6379 # Redis exposed on default Docker host machine IP address and the specified ports, e.g.: Expose redis to all network interfaces with docker run command or use 'docker-compose' for multihost setup (e.g., EXPOSE port1:port2)
# Copy your application source code into image – this will be useful if you want a specific version of the app running in development, but not necessary when using Docker to build and run containers as it already includes all dependencies specified by requirements files or other packages installed via pip. Uncomment below line (e.g., COPY . /app)
COPY ./src/main.* ../ # Your application source code goes here e.g.: Copy your main python file into the Docker image and set it as an entrypoint for docker run command or use 'docker-compose' to start multiple containers from this one (e.g., COPY . /app)
# Set default container startup mode – you can choose between "run" if running in a non-interactive way, like with `./start_all` script and using the Docker Compose file for multihost setup or use 'docker stack deploy' to start multiple containers from this one (e.g., CMD)
CMD ["python", "-u"] # Set default command that will be run when starting container e.g.: Default python entrypoint, you can change it if needed and set as an argument for docker-compose or use 'docker stack deploy' to start multiple containers from this one (e.g., CMD)
